findings.description: Off-by-one: range(total_chunks - 1) skips the final chunk, so every reassembled file is silently truncated/corrupted.
Path traversal: client-controlled session.filename is joined unsanitized, so '../' or an absolute path lets an attacker write/overwrite files outside UPLOAD_DIR.
Path traversal via client-controlled upload_id in the chunk filename allows writing chunk data outside CHUNK_DIR.
verify_checksum reads the whole file into memory via f.read(), risking memory exhaustion on files up to/over MAX_FILE_MB; should stream in blocks.
No per-chunk or cumulative size cap is enforced during receive; MAX_FILE_MB is never checked in the upload flow, enabling disk-exhaustion DoS.
Handler is not thread-safe: _sessions dict and the received set are mutated without locking, racing under concurrent requests typical for upload servers.
assemble writes directly to the final path and deletes chunks+session before any checksum/size verification, so a corrupt or oversize file is committed and cannot be reassembled.
start() overwrites an existing session for the same upload_id without cleaning prior chunk files, orphaning them and allowing session clobbering.
receive_chunk declares a bool return but raises on I/O errors (e.g. disk full, invalid path) instead of returning False, violating its contract.
is_complete only checks the in-memory received set, not on-disk presence, so a chunk deleted externally triggers FileNotFoundError mid-assembly leaving a partial dest file.
File-type validation relies solely on extension; no content/MIME inspection, so disguised payloads with allowed extensions pass.
Checksum comparison is not case-normalized; an uppercase client-supplied hex digest spuriously mismatches the lowercase hexdigest().
open(dest_path,'wb') follows symlinks, so a pre-planted symlink at the destination could redirect the write to overwrite a sensitive file.
Digest comparison is not constant-time; low practical relevance since the hash is an integrity check, not an authentication secret.
Hardcoded absolute storage paths reduce portability and testability; should be configurable.
Size limit named MAX_FILE_MB but computed with 1024*1024 (MiB); unit naming is inconsistent.
